feedingProfile.preferredNpk: balanced (e.g. 10-10-10) at planting, then bloom formula higher in phosphorus than nitrogen; in containers RHS advises weekly high-potassium liquid feed in summer

commonIssues: Root rot from overwatering or soggy soil — begonias are highly susceptible; ensure drainage holes and let soil dry slightly between waterings
Powdery mildew and Botrytis blight — water at the base of the plant early in the day and keep good air circulation
Mealybugs, thrips, spider mites, slugs and snails; may be sensitive to insecticidal soaps
Leaf scorch on green-leaved cultivars in hot afternoon sun — bronze-leaved cultivars tolerate full sun better
Tender annual: plant out only after frost danger passes and soil reaches at least 15-16 C (60 F); cold soil causes stunting

source.citation: North Carolina State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Begonia, Wax Types: full sun (6+ h) to deep shade (<2 h) tolerated, bronze-leaved best in sun; mounded fibrous-rooted habit 6-12 in; hardy zones 9-11; tolerant of hot summers and mild drought; mealybugs, thrips, rot, fungal diseases; excellent in containers and hanging baskets

University of Minnesota Extension — Begonia: prefer shade, morning sun only; ~1 inch of water per week, let soil dry slightly, highly susceptible to root rot; fertilize monthly with higher-phosphorus bloom formula after balanced fertilizer at planting; transplant out once soil reaches 60 F; toxic to cats and dogs
University of Minnesota Extension — Begonia: fertilize monthly after planting with formula higher in phosphorus than nitrogen to promote blooms; balanced NPK worked in at planting

Clemson University HGIC — Begonia factsheet: green-leaved cultivars best in morning sun/afternoon shade, bronze-leaved take full sun; keep soil moist but well drained; not heavy feeders (slow-release annual fertilizer); plant after frost danger; pests include spider mites, mealybugs, thrips, slugs; diseases include Botrytis, stem rot, powdery mildew

RHS — Begonias Outdoors (Summer Colour): sun or partial shade; water regularly in dry periods, avoid wetting foliage; feed weekly with high-potassium feed in containers through September; tender — plant out after frost danger; semperflorens potted on into 13 cm (5 in) pots

Illinois Extension — Wax Begonia, Fibrous Begonia: tender annual, wait for warm soil and air (cold soil stunts); full sun to shade once established with moisture; 6-12 in mature height; constant bloom May through October; moist, well-drained, high-organic-matter soil

Confidence note: baseIntervalDays=3 is derived from UMN's ~1 inch/week with soil allowed to dry slightly, tightened for containers; hotWeatherMultiplier and rainSkipThresholdMm are conservative planner estimates, not stated by any source

Confidence note: recommendedMinLiters=2 converts RHS's final 13 cm (5 in) pot per plant (~1 L) upward to a conservative single-plant container; ideal 3-10 L for fuller plants/mixed containers is an estimate. heatStressC=35 estimated from NCSU 'tolerant of hot summer temperatures' plus RHS note of foliage scorch in intense heat
